questions.text: What is the mathematical consequence of high multicollinearity on the linear regression parameter estimation?
options: It decreases the coefficient of determination (R^2) of the primary regression model.
It makes the feature covariance matrix (X^T * X) ill-conditioned, leading to highly unstable parameter weights with inflated variances.
It forces the model to use exponential decay loss functions instead of MSE.
It prevents the use of regularized L1 and L2 penalty terms.
questions.correct_answer: It makes the feature covariance matrix (X^T * X) ill-conditioned, leading to highly unstable parameter weights with inflated variances.
questions.explanation: When features are highly collinear, the columns of X are nearly linearly dependent, making (X^T * X) nearly singular. Its inverse matrix has extremely large elements, inflating the variance of our estimated coefficients.

questions.text: How can an engineer prevent preprocessing leakage when deploying feature scalers in cross-validation loops?
options: Perform scaling globally across all rows before splitting.
Wrap the scaler and estimator steps inside a Pipeline, fitting the scaler strictly on active training folds.
Use Min-Max scaling instead of Standard scaling.
Enable low_memory execution during scaling conversions.
questions.correct_answer: Wrap the scaler and estimator steps inside a Pipeline, fitting the scaler strictly on active training folds.
questions.explanation: Wrapping preprocessing and modeling inside a Pipeline guarantees that transformers are fit exclusively on the active training splits, applying the transformation parameters (like mean and variance) to validation splits without leaking validation statistics.
